content.problemStatement:
You are given two points A=(x1,y1,z1)A = (x_1, y_1, z_1)A=(x1​,y1​,z1​) and B=(x2,y2,z2)B = (x_2, y_2, z_2)B=(x2​,y2​,z2​) in the 3D coordinate system.

You would like to start at point AAA and reach point BBB.

To do this, you can move in any of the 666 cardinal directions — up, down, left, right, front or back — one step at a time.

Formally, in one step, you can choose exactly one coordinate and either increase or decrease it by 111.

However, you are not allowed to move more than KKK steps **consecutively** in one direction.

Your task is to determine the minimum number of steps required to move from AAA to BBB.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains 777 space-separated integers — the values x1,y1,z1,x2,y2,z2,Kx_1, y_1, z_1, x_2, y_2, z_2, Kx1​,y1​,z1​,x2​,y2​,z2​,K in order — denoting the coordinates of AAA, the coordinates of BBB, and the parameter KKK.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line a single integer: the answer to the problem.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: You want to move from (1,2,3)(1, 2, 3)(1,2,3) to (6,2,3)(6, 2, 3)(6,2,3), while not moving more than K=1K = 1K=1 time consecutively, meaning the same move can't be made twice in a row.
This requires 999 moves at minimum, here is one valid sequence:
(1,2,3)→(2,2,3)→(2,3,3)→(3,3,3)→(3,3,4)→(4,3,4)→(4,2,4)→(5,2,4)→(5,2,3)→(6,2,3)(1, 2, 3) \to (2, 2, 3) \to (2, 3, 3) \to (3, 3, 3) \to (3, 3, 4) \to (4, 3, 4) \to (4, 2, 4) \to (5, 2, 4) \to (5, 2, 3) \to (6, 2, 3)(1,2,3)→(2,2,3)→(2,3,3)→(3,3,3)→(3,3,4)→(4,3,4)→(4,2,4)→(5,2,4)→(5,2,3)→(6,2,3).
You can verify that the same move was never made twice in a row.
Test case 222: You want to move from (1,1,1)(1, 1, 1)(1,1,1) to (2,2,2)(2, 2, 2)(2,2,2).
You can move 111 step in the positive xxx-direction, 111 step in the positive yyy-direction, and 111 step in the positive zzz-direction, resulting in a total of 333 moves.
